1) INTRODUCTION 
a) Product Name  
 
OMI_HEALTH_BLOOM_BALTIC_spatiotemporal_coverage 
b) Indicator Evaluation Method 
The CMEMS Ocean Colour OMIs are derived from the reprocessed (MY) regional 
gridded chlorophyll products generated and distributed by the CMEMS OC-TAC. The 
input dataset are the spectral remote sensing reflectances (Rrs) produced by the 
Plymouth Marine Laboratory (PML) using an ad-hoc configuration for CMEMS of the ESA 
OC-CCI processor version 6 (OC- CCIv6) to merge at 1km resolution (rather than at 4km 
as for OC-CCI) MERIS, MODIS-AQUA, SeaWiFS, NPP-VIIRS and OLCI-A data. The 
chlorophyll product is derived from a Multi-Layer Perceptron neural-net (MLP) 
developed on field measurements collected within the BiOMaP program of JRC/EC 
(Zibordi et al., 2011). The algorithm is an ensemble of different MLPs that use Rrs at 
different wavelengths as input. The processing chain and the techniques used to 
develop the algorithm are detailed in Brando et al. (2021; 2024). 
 
In the Baltic Sea, the phytoplankton blooms occurring during summer are dominated 
by nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria. These blooms can have subsurface and/or surface 
accumulations, depending on the age of the bloom. Consistent with the HELCOM 
environmental reporting (e.g. Öberg 2018) the cyanobacterial subsurface and surface 
summer blooms are detected by applying the thresholds on Rrs at the wavelength of 
555 and 670 nm (Hansson and Håkansson 2007). The thresholds applied to daily Rrs 
images were set at Rrs (555) > 4.25 10−3 sr−1 and Rrs (670) > 1.22 10−3 sr−1 for the 
subsurface and surface blooms, respectively, as defined in Kahru et al (2007). This Rrs-
based approach to detect cyanobacterial blooms allows overcoming the difficulty of 
retrieving accurate Chla values in summer bloom conditions (Hansson and Håkansson 
2007; Brando et al 2021).  
 
The chlorophyll dataset of the Baltic Sea MY contain the cyanobacteria summer bloom 
mask variable (CYANOBLOOM), which has the same dimension as the chlorophyll data 
and reports whether the thresholds for the subsurface and surface blooms was 
exceeded on the daily Rrs images. As cyanobacterial blooms are extremely patchy 
and the surface scum is unlikely to totally cover a 1 km resolution pixel, it is possible for 
both thresholds to be exceeded concurrently. 
 
Product: OMI_HEALTH_BLOOM_BALTIC_spatiotemporal_coverage 
This product includes the time series of the total coverage of surface and subsurface 
blooms in summer for the whole basin on annual basis. Following Hansson and 
Håkansson (2007), the cyanobacteria summer bloom mask variable (CYANOBLOOM) 
in the MY regional chlorophyll dataset was aggregated from from June to September 
(i.e., days 161 to 270) of each year for the three possible values (subsurface =1; 
surface=2, and 3: both subsurface and surface summer blooms detected). Then the 
cumulative maps for every year for the three bloom classes were summarized as a 
spatiotemporal coverage and expressed in day km2.
